intent: A confined web service serving content from an NFS mount: httpd_t denied read+getattr on an nfs_t file. Network-filesystem labeling case. audit2why reports this is governed by the httpd_use_nfs boolean - the canonical remedy is the boolean, not a raw allow, which makes the narrow-allow suggestion technically valid but operationally inferior.
_kinds_note: authoritative_category in {TeAllowable, Constraint, Bounds} (libsepol/audit2why replay); floor_label in {none, Permissive, MlsSuspected, RoleSuspected} (record-only heuristic). NOTE: audit2why additionally reports a BOOLEAN remedy (httpd_use_nfs) - the access is TE-allowable but the shipped policy already has a tunable for it.

oracle.audit2allow_N: allow httpd_t nfs_t:file { getattr read }; (with a comment: 'This avc can be allowed using one of these booleans: httpd_use_nfs, git_system_use_nfs')
oracle.audit2allow_R: allow httpd_t nfs_t:file { getattr read }; (same raw allow + the same boolean comment; no interface macro substituted)
oracle.narrower_than_R_by: EQUAL raw allow in this case (no interface macro fired for nfs_t:file). Both -N and -R additionally PRINT a boolean hint as a comment. expected.te is exactly the raw allow. The triage value here is recognizing the boolean remedy: a raw allow works but `setsebool -P httpd_use_nfs 1` is the shipped, narrower-than-a-custom-module fix.

triage_expectation.suspected_trap: A naive impl that only parses the `allow ...` line and ignores the audit2why/audit2allow boolean comment would emit a custom-module allow when a shipped boolean (httpd_use_nfs) is the correct, maintained remedy. triage should detect the boolean hint and prefer it. Also: the perms must NOT be padded - nfs_t:file gets exactly { read getattr }.
